choices.text: The protests prove that Russia is a fully consolidated democracy because citizens were permitted to demonstrate publicly.
choices.explanation: Permitting some protest does not indicate democratic consolidation; Russia's regime systematically suppresses opposition through arrests and legal restrictions, and the CED classifies Russia as a managed democracy or hybrid regime, not a consolidated democracy.

choices.text: The legislation represents an example of the Russian government using a corporatist interest group system to channel civic demands.
choices.explanation: Corporatism involves state-licensed associations channeling societal interests; the passage describes state legislation opposed by civil society groups — this is the state suppressing civil society, not a corporatist integration of its demands.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government
questions.question: Which of the following best describes the purpose of Mexico's National Electoral Institute (INE)?

choices.text: To vet candidates before elections and exclude those who do not support the ruling party's platform.
choices.explanation: This describes Iran's Guardian Council function; Mexico's INE was created to administer elections free from partisan control, not to ideologically screen candidates.

choices.text: To run elections free from partisan control, reducing fraud and building confidence in results.
choices.explanation: Per DEM-2.B.4.b and PAU-4.A.4, Mexico created the INE (formerly IFE) as part of its democratic transition specifically to reduce voter fraud, manipulation, and to build confidence in electoral results.

choices.text: To allocate proportional representation seats to parties based on national vote totals.
choices.explanation: While the INE oversees the PR seat allocation process administratively, its broader purpose is independent election administration to reduce fraud and strengthen democratic competition — not simply to perform the mathematical PR calculation.

choices.text: To serve as an upper legislative chamber that provides consultative review of legislation passed by the Chamber of Deputies.
choices.explanation: The INE is an electoral management body, not a legislative chamber; Mexico's upper chamber is the Senate — these are structurally and functionally distinct institutions.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Under China's electoral system for the National People's Congress, voters at the local level elect delegates to county-level congresses, who then elect delegates to provincial congresses, who then elect NPC members. At each level, the Communist Party screens and approves candidates before they appear on the ballot."

questions.question: Which of the following best explains how the electoral system described in the passage differs from the directly elected lower chambers found in the UK, Mexico, Nigeria, and Iran?

choices.text: China's system uses proportional representation at each level, while the UK, Mexico, Nigeria, and Iran all use plurality systems.
choices.explanation: China's NPC selection is an indirect election through layered bodies, not a PR system; the distinction is between direct popular election and indirect selection through intermediate delegates.

choices.text: China's multi-tier indirect election insulates the NPC from direct popular expression; the others hold direct elections even where candidate access is restricted.
choices.explanation: Per DEM-2.A.1.a, China's NPC is selected through a series of local and regional elections that insulate outcomes from direct citizen choice; even Iran's vetted direct elections give citizens a direct vote — China's layering adds structural distance from popular will.

choices.text: China's system produces the same outcomes as a direct election because CPC candidate screening occurs identically in all direct election countries.
choices.explanation: CPC screening at every level of an indirect system is structurally different from Iran's single-stage Guardian Council vetting; the layered indirect structure in China creates multiple filtering steps unavailable in direct election systems.

choices.text: China's NPC members serve shorter terms than directly elected legislators in the other countries because indirect elections happen more frequently.
choices.explanation: NPC term length is not the relevant distinction the CED draws; the direct versus indirect election structure — and the CPC's role in controlling it — is the key comparison point.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

rows: Threshold raised from 4% to 5% for PR seats
Gender quota added to party list system
Geographic threshold added for presidential winners
Devolution of powers to Scotland, Wales, N. Ireland

questions.question: According to the table, which of the following best describes how election rule changes in Russia differ from those in Mexico and Nigeria?

choices.text: Russia's changes expanded representation for minority groups, while Mexico's and Nigeria's changes reduced it.
choices.explanation: The table shows the opposite: Russia's threshold increase reduced small-party access, while Mexico's gender quota and Nigeria's geographic threshold were intended to expand or ensure representation.

choices.text: Russia's rule changes entrenched United Russia by narrowing competitive access; Mexico's and Nigeria's were designed to broaden representation.
choices.explanation: Per DEM-2.B.6 and PAU-4.A.3, Russia raised thresholds and eliminated gubernatorial elections to entrench United Russia's dominance; Mexico added gender quotas to increase women's representation and Nigeria's geographic threshold was designed to ensure broad national mandates.

choices.text: All three countries made the same type of rule changes — raising thresholds — but with different effects on representation.
choices.explanation: Russia raised thresholds; Mexico added gender quotas; Nigeria added geographic requirements — these are structurally different types of rule changes, not variations on the same mechanism.

choices.text: Mexico's gender quota and Nigeria's geographic threshold are both forms of managed democracy because they restrict free electoral competition.
choices.explanation: Managed democracy involves systematic manipulation to ensure incumbent dominance; gender quotas and geographic thresholds serve representational goals (women's inclusion, cross-regional mandates) within legitimate democratic frameworks — they are not tools of authoritarian entrenchment.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Before Mexico's democratic reforms of the 1990s, the president of Mexico effectively chose the next PRI presidential candidate through the practice known as el dedazo — the figurative pointing of the finger. This informal tradition, combined with patronage networks embedded in state-owned enterprises and official labor unions, meant that PRI dominance was self-reinforcing: the president controlled who ran, who won party endorsement, and which organizations received state resources."

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why eliminating el dedazo was necessary but not sufficient to produce genuine multiparty competition in Mexico?

choices.text: Eliminating el dedazo was sufficient on its own because presidential candidate selection was the only mechanism of PRI dominance.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-4.A.4, PRI dominance rested on multiple pillars — patronage networks, state enterprise control, corporatist organizations, and biased electoral administration — not solely on the dedazo candidate selection practice.

choices.text: Removing el dedazo eliminated candidate-selection control but left PRI's patronage networks, state enterprises, and corporatist structures intact until further reforms.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-4.A.4, Mexico's transition required four mutually reinforcing reforms: eliminating el dedazo, privatizing state enterprises, decentralizing power, and creating the INE/IFE; any single reform alone would have left PRI's other structural advantages intact.

choices.text: El dedazo was not actually important to PRI dominance; the party maintained control primarily through proportional representation electoral rules that advantaged incumbents.
choices.explanation: El dedazo was explicitly listed by the CED as a key mechanism of PRI control; Mexico's mixed electoral system with PR existed during PRI dominance and did not prevent it — the dedazo was important to internal party discipline and candidate selection.

choices.text: Eliminating el dedazo made Mexico's party system identical to China's because both countries then relied on indirect candidate selection.
choices.explanation: Eliminating el dedazo moved Mexico toward competitive democratic candidate selection; China's indirect NPC selection remains CPC-controlled — the two systems moved in opposite directions, not toward equivalence.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government
questions.question: Which of the following best describes the concept of el dedazo in Mexican politics?

choices.text: A constitutional provision requiring the Mexican president to serve a single six-year term.
choices.explanation: El dedazo was an informal practice, not a constitutional provision; Mexico's single six-year term (sexenio) is a constitutional rule distinct from the informal candidate selection practice.

choices.text: The informal practice by which the incumbent PRI president personally selected the next PRI presidential candidate.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-4.A.4, el dedazo (the pointing finger) was the informal mechanism by which Mexico's PRI president handpicked the next presidential nominee, bypassing internal party competition and concentrating succession power in the incumbent.

choices.text: A proportional representation rule that allocated party list seats based on each region's share of national population.
choices.explanation: El dedazo has nothing to do with seat allocation; it was a candidate succession practice within the PRI, not an electoral formula for distributing legislative seats.

choices.text: An ethnic quota system that reserved seats in Mexico's Congress for indigenous communities.
choices.explanation: El dedazo was a party succession mechanism, not an ethnic quota; Mexico's gender quotas (not ethnic quotas) are the CED's noted representational mechanism in the party list system.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "A comparative politics scholar argues: 'The same institution — an election — serves fundamentally different functions depending on the regime type. In a democracy, elections transfer power and hold governments accountable. In an authoritarian system, elections are theatrical performances that generate a veneer of popular consent without genuine accountability.'"

questions.question: Which of the following course-country comparisons best illustrates the author's argument?

choices.text: Mexico 2000 (PAN defeats PRI) versus Russia's managed elections illustrates genuine power transfer against engineered incumbent legitimation.
choices.explanation: Mexico's 2000 election produced genuine power transfer after 71 years of PRI rule; Russia's elections consistently produce United Russia victories through systematic manipulation — exactly the democratic accountability versus legitimating function the author describes.

choices.text: Comparing the UK's House of Lords with Russia's Federation Council illustrates the difference between democratic and authoritarian elections.
choices.explanation: Neither the House of Lords nor the Federation Council is elected; these are appointed bodies — comparing them does not address the author's argument about elections serving different functions.

choices.text: Comparing Nigeria's geographic threshold with Mexico's gender quota illustrates elections that transfer power versus elections that legitimate incumbents.
choices.explanation: Both Nigeria's threshold and Mexico's gender quota are rules within functioning democratic or democratizing systems; neither illustrates the authoritarian legitimation function the author describes.

choices.text: Comparing China's indirect NPC elections with Iran's directly elected but vetted Majles illustrates elections that transfer power versus elections used for theatrical legitimation.
choices.explanation: Both China and Iran use their electoral processes for legitimation rather than genuine power transfer — this comparison shows variation within authoritarian systems, not the democratic-versus-authoritarian contrast the author draws.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

rows: PM: commands House of Commons majority

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why Nigeria's executive election rule is uniquely designed for its country compared to all other course countries?

choices.text: Nigeria is the only course country that requires a runoff election when no candidate reaches an absolute majority in the first round.
choices.explanation: Iran and Russia both require absolute majorities with runoffs; Nigeria does not use a formal runoff — its unique feature is the geographic distribution requirement, not a runoff rule.

choices.text: Nigeria uniquely combines a national plurality with a sub-national geographic distribution requirement, reflecting its multiethnic federal character.
choices.explanation: Per DEM-2.B.3.b, Nigeria's 25%-in-two-thirds-of-states rule is unique among course countries; no other country combines a national plurality threshold with a sub-national geographic distribution requirement — it is a direct product of Nigeria's federal, multi-ethnic structure.

choices.text: Nigeria uses an indirect election process like China's NPC system, which makes its executive selection uniquely insulated from popular will.
choices.explanation: Nigeria holds a direct popular presidential election; China's NPC election is indirect — these are opposite approaches to executive selection.

choices.text: Nigeria is unique because its independent electoral commission (INEC) must ratify all presidential candidates before the election, like Iran's Guardian Council.
choices.explanation: Nigeria's INEC is an independent electoral management body that administers elections; it does not vet candidates on ideological grounds as Iran's Guardian Council does — these bodies serve different purposes.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "A political scientist observes that strong civil society — the network of voluntary associations, NGOs, and social movements operating between the state and the individual — tends to correlate with higher levels of democratization. Countries with vibrant civil societies tend to have more competitive elections, stronger rule of law, and greater protection of civil liberties."

questions.question: Which of the following course countries best illustrates the relationship described in the passage?

choices.text: China, because its mass organizations provide broad civil society participation in policy making.
choices.explanation: China's mass organizations are state-directed instruments, not autonomous civil society; state-directed organizations that transmit party directives are not civil society in the sense the passage describes.

choices.text: United Kingdom, where autonomous associations, a free press, and social movements operate within a consolidated democracy.
choices.explanation: Per IEF-2 and the Country Matrix for civil society, the UK is the primary example of strong civil society reinforcing democratic governance; autonomous groups, free press, and competitive elections are mutually reinforcing in a consolidated democracy.

choices.text: Russia, because the high number of registered political parties indicates vibrant civil society.
choices.explanation: Formally registered but suppressed parties do not constitute vibrant civil society; Russia's managed democracy systematically restricts independent civil society through NGO restrictions and media control.

choices.text: Iran, because the Green Movement demonstrated that civil society can emerge and challenge the regime effectively.
choices.explanation: The Green Movement was suppressed; Iran's Guardian Council, IRGC, and restricted press represent a state that systematically constrains civil society — one protest movement does not indicate vibrant civil society overall.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Boko Haram emerged in northeastern Nigeria in the early 2000s as a movement opposing Western education and secular governance. It expanded into an armed insurgency seeking to establish an Islamic state in northern Nigeria, conducting mass kidnappings, bombings, and territorial occupation. At its peak it controlled territory the size of Belgium in Borno State before a multinational military coalition and Nigerian forces reduced its territorial control."

questions.question: Which of the following best explains how the CED classifies Boko Haram as a social movement despite its extreme violence?

choices.text: The CED classifies Boko Haram as a social movement because it began as a nonviolent organization and only became violent after government repression.
choices.explanation: The CED does not require a nonviolent origin for social movement classification; the defining criterion is large-group collective action for broad social or political change — which Boko Haram meets regardless of its founding tactics.

choices.text: Establishing an Islamic state is broad societal transformation pursued through mass mobilization — meeting IEF-2.A.1's definition regardless of violent methods.
choices.explanation: Per IEF-2.A.1, social movements involve large groups pushing collectively for significant political or social change; Boko Haram's broad goal of establishing an Islamic state and its mass mobilization fit this definition, and IEF-2.A.5 acknowledges that some social movements use militancy.

choices.text: The CED classifies Boko Haram as an interest group because it advocates for a specific policy — the implementation of Sharia law — rather than broad social change.
choices.explanation: The CED explicitly classifies Boko Haram as a social movement in IEF-2.A.3.d; its goal of establishing an Islamic state is a broad societal transformation, not a narrow single-issue policy advocacy characteristic of interest groups.

choices.text: The CED does not classify Boko Haram at all because it is a terrorist organization beyond the scope of comparative politics analysis.
choices.explanation: The CED explicitly names Boko Haram in IEF-2.A.3.d as a social movement example in Nigeria — it is within scope as a course example of the social movement category.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "A scholar argues that authoritarian regimes sometimes open up controlled access to interest groups and civic organizations for strategic reasons: doing so provides the regime with information about societal grievances, manages potential unrest by providing a legitimate outlet, and creates the appearance of responsiveness without conceding genuine political competition."

questions.question: Which of the following course-country examples best illustrates the strategic rationale described in the passage?

choices.text: China's eight minor parties offer the CPC consultation cover without any threat to its governing monopoly.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-4.A.2, China allows eight minor parties to exist to broaden discussion and consultation; this fits the scholar's argument — the regime gains information and the appearance of multi-party consultation while retaining exclusive power.

choices.text: Mexico's creation of the INE, which gave opposition parties a genuine pathway to competitive elections and ultimately to defeating the PRI.
choices.explanation: Mexico's INE was a genuine democratic reform that created conditions for actual power transfer; this does not illustrate strategic controlled access as described — it led to real competition and PRI's defeat in 2000.

choices.text: Nigeria's ethnic quota system, which mandates party representation across ethnic groups to prevent any single group from dominating.
choices.explanation: Nigeria's ethnic quotas are a representational mechanism in a democratizing multi-ethnic system, not a strategic authoritarian tool to manage dissent while preventing genuine competition.

choices.text: The UK's House of Lords, which provides appointed representation to manage opposition without allowing voters to directly choose legislators.
choices.explanation: The UK is a consolidated democracy; the House of Lords is an appointed review chamber within a democratic system, not an authoritarian regime's strategic tool to manage societal grievances.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content:
"The diagram below represents a simplified linkage model.

CITIZENS → [Political Party] → [Legislature/Executive] → POLICY

In this model, political parties serve as the intermediary that translates citizen preferences into policy outcomes. The strength of this linkage depends on how well parties organize citizens, how responsive legislators are to party platforms, and how effectively parties can actually enact policy once in power."

questions.question: Using the linkage model, which of the following best compares citizen-to-policy linkage in the United Kingdom and Iran?

choices.text: UK party discipline reliably translates platforms into policy; Iran's shifting factions and Guardian Council vetting weaken every link in the chain.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-4.A.7 and PAU-4.B.1.b, the UK's strong party discipline ensures reliable platform-to-policy translation; Iran's loosely formed alliances lack organizational capacity to aggregate citizen preferences, and the Guardian Council pre-filters who can stand — weakening every link in the chain.

choices.text: Linkage is stronger in Iran because the Guardian Council ensures only qualified candidates participate, producing a more competent legislature.
choices.explanation: Competence of legislators is not the criterion in the linkage model; the Guardian Council's vetting is based on ideological compliance, not competence, and it reduces the pool of candidates who can translate citizen preferences into policy.

choices.text: Linkage is identical in both countries because both hold elections to fill their legislative chambers.
choices.explanation: The holding of elections alone does not determine linkage strength; the quality of party organization, the freedom of candidate selection, and the responsiveness of legislators to citizen preferences all differ markedly between the UK and Iran.

choices.text: Linkage is weaker in the UK because FPTP produces wasted votes, meaning many citizens' preferences are not represented.
choices.explanation: While FPTP does produce wasted votes, the UK's strong party discipline still ensures that parties that do win create reliable policy linkage for represented constituencies; this drawback does not make UK linkage weaker than Iran's systematically disrupted chain.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government
questions.question: Which of the following best describes a single peak association (SPA) in a corporatist interest group system?

choices.text: An independent civil society organization that competes with other groups for government attention without any official state recognition.
choices.explanation: This describes a pluralist interest group; SPAs in corporatist systems are state-sanctioned and officially recognized — they do not compete freely with unlimited other groups.

choices.text: The sole state-licensed organization per sector, with exclusive authority to channel that sector's interests into policy.
choices.explanation: Per IEF-2.B.2, a single peak association is the one officially recognized and licensed group per sector in a corporatist system — one official labor federation, one business association — giving the state control over who represents each sector.

choices.text: A political party that forms a coalition with other parties to secure a legislative majority.
choices.explanation: An SPA is an interest group organization, not a political party; it represents a societal sector in policy advocacy, not in electoral competition for legislative seats.

choices.text: A geographic constituency represented by a single legislator under FPTP rules.
choices.explanation: This describes a single-member district, an electoral constituency concept; an SPA is an organized interest representation entity in a corporatist system, entirely separate from electoral geography.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "The People's Democratic Party (PDP) dominated Nigerian national politics from 1999, when Nigeria returned to civilian rule, until 2015, when the All Progressives Congress (APC) defeated PDP incumbent Goodluck Jonathan. Political observers note that the APC's victory required building an unprecedented coalition of former opponents of the PDP across Nigeria's regions and ethnic communities."

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why the APC's coalition-building strategy was necessary given Nigeria's electoral rules?

choices.text: Nigeria's proportional representation system required the APC to earn at least 5% of the national vote to win legislative seats, making a broad coalition essential to cross the threshold.
choices.explanation: Nigeria's House of Representatives uses FPTP, not PR with a national threshold; the relevant rule for presidential elections is the 25%-in-two-thirds-of-states geographic threshold, not a PR seat threshold.

choices.text: Nigeria's 25%-in-two-thirds-of-states rule made building a geographically diverse, cross-ethnic coalition structurally necessary to win.
choices.explanation: Per DEM-2.B.3.b, Nigeria's geographic threshold requires a winning candidate to demonstrate cross-regional support; the APC's need to build a multi-regional coalition directly reflects this structural electoral requirement.

choices.text: Nigeria's runoff system forced the APC to broaden its appeal between the first and second rounds of the 2015 election.
choices.explanation: Nigeria does not use a runoff system; the geographic threshold is met in a single round, not through a formal second-round runoff process.

choices.text: Nigeria's Guardian Council required all presidential candidates to demonstrate cross-regional support before approving their candidacy.
choices.explanation: Nigeria has no Guardian Council; it is Iran that uses candidate vetting; Nigeria's INEC administers elections without ideological vetting, and the geographic requirement is an electoral outcome threshold, not a pre-election candidacy screen.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Mexico's Chamber of Deputies allocates 200 of its 500 seats through a party list proportional representation system. Parties submit ranked lists of candidates, and seats are distributed in proportion to each party's national vote share. Gender parity requirements apply to the party lists, mandating equal numbers of men and women."

questions.question: Which of the following best explains how the PR list system described in the passage serves a different representational objective than the 300 single-member district seats in the same chamber?

choices.text: The PR seats provide geographic constituency accountability by linking specific candidates to defined districts, while the SMD seats promote national party vote-share representation.
choices.explanation: This reverses the functions: SMD seats link specific candidates to geographic districts; PR list seats provide national vote-share representation without constituency links.

choices.text: PR seats broaden party and demographic representation (including women via parity rules); SMD seats provide direct geographic accountability.
choices.explanation: Per DEM-2.A.1.c and DEM-2.B.1, the 200 PR seats enable minority parties and women to achieve representation proportional to national vote share; the 300 SMD seats provide individual district accountability — the two components serve complementary but distinct representational goals.

choices.text: Both components serve identical representational functions; the split exists only to increase total chamber size.
choices.explanation: The two components serve structurally different representational purposes; Mexico deliberately designed its mixed system to balance direct constituency representation (SMD) with broader party and demographic representation (PR).

choices.text: The PR seats reduce party competition by concentrating power in the top of party hierarchies who control list rankings.
choices.explanation: While party leadership does control list rankings, the question asks which representational objective the PR component serves — which is broader demographic and party representation, not reduced competition.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content:
"The table below compares interest group systems across course countries."

| Country | System Type | State Control Over Access | Example |
|---------|------------|--------------------------|--------|
| United Kingdom | Pluralist | Low | Multiple competing labor unions |
| Mexico (post-2000) | Transitional toward pluralist | Medium and declining | Former PRI-linked unions now compete with independents |
| Mexico (PRI era) | Corporatist | High | CTM (official labor federation) |
| China | State-directed | Very high | ACFTU (All-China Federation of Trade Unions — state body) |
| Iran | Corporatist/authoritarian | High | Basij, state-sponsored groups |

questions.question: Which of the following conclusions is best supported by comparing Mexico (PRI era) with China in the table?

choices.text: Both show high state control, but China transmits directives downward through groups while Mexico's SPAs nominally channeled sectoral interests upward.
choices.explanation: Per IEF-2.B.2, corporatism channels real societal interests through licensed associations; China's ACFTU transmits CPC directives, not genuine labor interests — the direction of information flow differs even though state control is high in both.

choices.text: Mexico's PRI-era corporatism is identical to China's system because both involved one-party governments controlling all interest group activity.
choices.explanation: Mexico under PRI was a dominant-party state with some pluralist tendencies, not a formal one-party state like China; its corporatist associations retained nominal sectoral identities even under PRI control.

choices.text: The table shows that corporatist systems always transition to pluralism over time, as demonstrated by both Mexico and China.
choices.explanation: Mexico transitioned toward pluralism after the PRI's decline; China has not made a comparable transition — one example does not constitute a universal trend.

choices.text: High state control over interest group access is always associated with democratic systems because it enables organized policy input.
choices.explanation: High state control is associated with authoritarian or dominant-party systems in the table; the UK's pluralist (low control) system is the democratic model, not the high-control systems.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Nigeria's multiparty system includes over 30 registered political parties, but national power has oscillated primarily between the People's Democratic Party (PDP) and the All Progressives Congress (APC). Ethnic and religious affiliations, regional loyalties, and federal constituency distribution all influence party competition in ways that differ markedly from the ideological left-right competition that structures politics in Western parliamentary systems."

questions.question: Which of the following best explains how ethnicity and federalism shape Nigeria's party system differently from the United Kingdom's?

choices.text: Nigeria's parties must aggregate across ethnic and regional lines in a federal system; UK parties compete on national ideological platforms.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-4.B.1.d, Nigeria's multi-ethnic federal structure means parties must navigate ethnic quotas, regional loyalty, and geographic threshold requirements; the UK's parties (Conservative, Labour) compete on national ideological platforms, shaped by FPTP's preference for broad geographic support.

choices.text: Nigeria's party competition is more ideological than the UK's because its parties have formal left-right platforms written into their constitutions.
choices.explanation: The passage specifically notes Nigeria's competition differs from ideological left-right competition; Nigerian party competition is characterized by ethnic, religious, and regional factors, not formal ideological platforms.

choices.text: The UK's party system is shaped by ethnic quotas like Nigeria's, because both countries have significant minority populations.
choices.explanation: The UK does not use ethnic quotas for party representation; ethnic quotas in the CED context are specific to Nigeria's multi-ethnic federal legislature, not to UK party competition.

choices.text: Nigeria's FPTP system produces the same two-party dynamic as the UK's, proving that electoral rules determine party competition regardless of ethnic or federal context.
choices.explanation: Nigeria also uses FPTP yet has a multiparty system with two dominant parties plus a significant third — demonstrating that electoral rules interact with social context, not that they deterministically produce identical party dynamics.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "A student argues: 'Because the United Kingdom uses first-past-the-post elections, it cannot have a multiparty system; FPTP always produces exactly two parties.'"

questions.question: Which of the following responses best refutes the student's claim using evidence from the course?

choices.text: The SNP and Plaid Cymru win Commons seats through geographic concentration, showing FPTP can support more than two parties regionally.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-4.B.1.h, single-member districts allow regional parties to win legislative seats where their vote is geographically concentrated; the UK's multi-national character creates conditions where FPTP rewards regional parties even as it discourages nationally spread minor parties.

choices.text: The UK uses proportional representation for most Commons seats, which explains why minor parties win representation.
choices.explanation: The UK's House of Commons uses exclusively FPTP; there is no PR component for Commons elections — the student's error is about the effect of FPTP, not about the electoral system itself.

choices.text: The UK's FPTP produces exactly two parties, confirming the student's claim; the SNP and other minor parties have never won House of Commons seats.
choices.explanation: The SNP has won dozens of Commons seats through geographic concentration; this is factually incorrect and confirms rather than refutes the student's overstated claim.

choices.text: FPTP always produces multiparty systems, which proves the student's claim is false — but is itself equally overstated.
choices.explanation: The claim that FPTP always produces multiparty systems is also an overstatement; FPTP tends to produce two-party systems nationally but allows regional party representation — neither blanket claim is accurate.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Russia's Federation Council is composed of two representatives from each of Russia's federal subjects — one appointed by the regional executive and one by the regional legislature. In addition, the creation of nine federal districts with presidential envoys has reasserted central control over regional governments, with the president able to influence who governs at the regional level."

questions.question: Which of the following best explains how Russia's Federation Council differs from Mexico's directly elected Senate in terms of regime function?

choices.text: Russia's appointed Federation Council extends presidential power over regions; Mexico's directly elected Senate checks executive authority.
choices.explanation: Per DEM-2.B.5.c, Russia's appointment process reasserts federal presidential power; Mexico's Senate (three seats per state including a minority-party seat) is directly elected — the two upper chambers serve opposite functions: one reinforcing executive power, one providing elected representation.

choices.text: Russia's Federation Council and Mexico's Senate perform identical functions because both represent subnational units.
choices.explanation: Representing subnational units is a shared structural feature, not a functional equivalence; the CED explicitly contrasts appointment systems serving regime interests (Russia) with elected chambers in democracies — form and function differ.

choices.text: Mexico's Senate is appointed by the president, while Russia's Federation Council is directly elected by regional voters.
choices.explanation: This is the opposite of the correct description: Mexico's senators are directly elected, while Russia's Federation Council members are appointed by regional governors and legislatures.

choices.text: Both chambers are appointed through the same process because both Russia and Mexico use federal systems.
choices.explanation: Federalism does not determine upper chamber composition method; Mexico is federal with a directly elected Senate, while Russia's federal structure pairs with an appointed Federation Council that serves different political purposes.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "A professor of comparative politics describes the concept of disproportionality: 'Disproportionality measures the gap between a party's vote share and its seat share. Under proportional representation, this gap is small — a party that wins 15% of the vote tends to win approximately 15% of seats. Under FPTP, this gap is often large — a party might win 40% of the vote but 55% of seats, while a third party might win 15% of the vote and only 3% of seats.'"

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why proportional representation tends to produce lower disproportionality than FPTP?

choices.text: PR allocates seats by national vote share, so every vote cast for a party contributes directly to its seat total regardless of where voters are located geographically.
choices.explanation: Per DEM-2.B.1 and DEM-2.A.2, PR's multimember districts and vote-share allocation ensure that a party's legislative representation closely matches its electoral support nationally; FPTP's single-winner districts reward geographic efficiency and penalize dispersed national support.

choices.text: PR produces lower disproportionality because it requires candidates to win an absolute majority in each district, preventing small pluralities from translating into outsized seat shares.
choices.explanation: PR does not require majority wins in individual districts; it allocates seats proportionally by party vote share across multimember districts — the mechanism is vote-share allocation, not a majority district requirement.

choices.text: FPTP produces lower disproportionality than PR because voters in single-member districts make their preferences very clear.
choices.explanation: FPTP consistently produces higher disproportionality, not lower; the passage and CED are explicit that FPTP amplifies the seat share of plurality winners relative to their vote share.

choices.text: PR produces lower disproportionality because threshold rules prevent small parties from winning any seats, concentrating representation among larger parties.
choices.explanation: Threshold rules in PR systems do create some disproportionality for parties below the threshold; but overall PR systems still produce much lower disproportionality than FPTP because parties above the threshold gain seats proportional to their vote share.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "The Green Movement in Iran and the Zapatista movement in Mexico both emerged from frustrated citizen demands that were not being addressed through existing political channels. Both mobilized large numbers of people around grievances related to political exclusion and economic inequality. Both also demonstrate the difficulty of sustaining social movement pressure over the long term."

questions.question: Which of the following best explains a key difference between these two movements in terms of their relationship to the state?

choices.text: The Green Movement faced an authoritarian regime with no legitimate opposition channels, while the Zapatistas operated in a democratizing Mexico that offered some negotiation pathways, giving the latter more institutional leverage.
choices.explanation: Per IEF-2.A.3.a–b, Iran's 2009 Green Movement faced a theocratic authoritarian regime with no legitimate opposition outlet; the Zapatistas operated in a Mexico undergoing democratic transition, where the government eventually engaged in negotiations and the movement achieved some political recognition.

choices.text: The Green Movement was a violent armed movement while the Zapatistas used exclusively nonviolent protest, explaining the difference in outcomes.
choices.explanation: The Green Movement was notably nonviolent; it was the Zapatistas who began with an armed uprising on January 1, 1994 — the characterizations of violent versus nonviolent are reversed.

choices.text: The Zapatistas failed to achieve any of their stated goals because the Mexican government used military force to crush the movement within days, while the Green Movement successfully overturned the 2009 Iranian election result through mass protest.
choices.explanation: Neither movement achieved its primary stated goal: the Zapatistas achieved some recognition but not full indigenous land rights; the Green Movement did not overturn the 2009 results and was suppressed.

choices.text: Both the Green Movement and the Zapatistas are better classified as interest groups, since each organized around specific policy grievances (election results and NAFTA respectively) rather than the broad societal transformation required of a true social movement.
choices.explanation: Both are classified as social movements in the CED; having specific triggering grievances does not make a movement an interest group — both mobilized large, diverse groups around broad social and political change agendas.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "A scholar of comparative electoral systems writes: 'Single-member district plurality systems have two key features that single-member-district proponents value: strong constituency accountability — one representative who is directly answerable to one geographic community — and a tendency to produce stable majority governments by amplifying the seat share of the plurality winner. Critics respond that these advantages come at the cost of representation: millions of voters whose preferred candidates lose in every district are effectively unrepresented in the legislature.'"

questions.question: Which of the following course countries provides the strongest empirical support for the critic's position described in the passage?

choices.text: Mexico's mixed system produces more multiparty representation than a pure FPTP system would — but since Mexico is not a pure FPTP case, it does not provide the direct FPTP evidence the critic's position requires.
choices.explanation: Mexico's mixed system supports the case for reform away from pure FPTP; but the critic's position is about FPTP's representational costs — Mexico does not use pure FPTP and so is not the strongest illustration.

choices.text: United Kingdom, where parties winning 35–45% of the national vote have secured parliamentary majorities, while parties with 10–15% of votes received only 1–2% of seats — millions of voters unrepresented.
choices.explanation: Per DEM-2.B.2, the UK's FPTP system classically produces high disproportionality — parties with dispersed national support (like UKIP in 2015) win negligible seats despite significant vote shares, directly embodying the critic's 'millions unrepresented' argument.

choices.text: Nigeria, because its geographic threshold means presidential votes for candidates who do not reach 25% in two-thirds of states effectively contribute nothing to the outcome — a type of wasted vote under FPTP logic.
choices.explanation: Nigeria's geographic threshold applies to presidential election qualification, not to legislative seat allocation; the representational cost the critic describes is about legislative FPTP seat-vote disproportionality, which is better illustrated by the UK.

choices.text: Russia, because its mixed system's PR component ensures any party exceeding the 5% threshold does receive proportional seats — making Russia's system actively reduce the representational cost the critic describes for FPTP.
choices.explanation: Russia's PR component actually reduces (not illustrates) the representational cost described; the critic's argument is about FPTP's representational failures, and Russia's PR element is designed to mitigate those problems.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government
questions.question: Which of the following best explains how Russia uses threshold rules as a tool of managed democracy?

choices.text: Russia's threshold rules require all presidential candidates to win an absolute majority in the first round, eliminating smaller candidates before runoffs.
choices.explanation: Presidential majority/runoff requirements are separate from threshold rules; thresholds apply to the PR portion of Duma elections, preventing parties from converting vote share into seats if they fall below the minimum.

choices.text: Raising the PR vote threshold eliminates smaller opposition parties from Duma representation, concentrating seats in parties favorable to the government.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-4.A.3, Russia's increasing threshold rules limit party access to the ballot; raising the PR threshold from 4% to 5% mathematically eliminates smaller opposition parties from Duma representation, concentrating seats in United Russia.

choices.text: Russia's threshold rules function like Mexico's gender quotas, ensuring a minimum level of underrepresented group access to the Duma.
choices.explanation: Russia's threshold rules restrict competitive access by eliminating small parties; Mexico's gender quotas expand representational access by guaranteeing women's inclusion — these serve opposite purposes.

choices.text: Threshold rules in Russia are a form of candidate vetting that screens individual Duma members before they appear on the ballot.
choices.explanation: Threshold rules set minimum vote shares for parties to win seats; candidate vetting screens individual people before elections — these are different mechanisms operating at different levels.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content:
"The table below shows election results for three course countries."

| Country | Party | Vote Share | Seat Share | Seat–Vote Gap |
|---------|-------|-----------|-----------|---------------|
| United Kingdom | Conservative | 43.6% | 56.2% | +12.6% |
| United Kingdom | Liberal Democrats | 11.6% | 1.7% | −9.9% |
| Mexico (PR list) | PAN | 18.2% | 18.0% | −0.2% |
| Russia | United Russia | 49.8% | 76.2% | +26.4% |
| Russia | CPRF | 19.2% | 9.5% | −9.7% |

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why Russia's United Russia shows a higher seat–vote gap (+26.4%) than the UK Conservative Party (+12.6%) despite both countries having mixed or FPTP systems?

choices.text: Russia's gap reflects a purely mechanical effect of FPTP geography, identical in nature to the UK's gap but larger due to Russia's greater geographic size.
choices.explanation: While geographic size may play some role, the CED attributes Russia's seat bonus to managed democracy tools — registration barriers, media control, and judicial disqualification — that go beyond the mechanical effects of FPTP.

choices.text: Russia's gap exceeds what FPTP mechanics alone explain; registration barriers, media exclusion, and court disqualifications inflate it further.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-4.A.3, Russia combines electoral system mechanics with managed democracy tools — the gap is inflated beyond what any neutral FPTP or mixed system would produce, because the entire competitive environment is tilted through non-mechanical political manipulation.

choices.text: The UK's Liberal Democrats' negative gap (−9.9%) proves that FPTP is a form of managed democracy in which the state deliberately suppresses minor parties.
choices.explanation: The Liberal Democrats' negative gap is a structural mechanical effect of FPTP applied to geographically dispersed support — not deliberate state suppression of a specific party; managed democracy involves intentional political manipulation, not structural electoral mathematics.

choices.text: Mexico's near-zero gap for PAN proves that PR systems always produce fully proportional outcomes with no seat–vote gap.
choices.explanation: Mexico's PR component produces near-proportional results for the 200 list seats, but Mexico's overall system has an SMD component too; claiming all PR systems produce zero gaps ignores threshold effects and other PR design variations.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government
questions.question: Which of the following best describes a characteristic of Iran's political party system?

choices.text: Iran has three dominant formal parties — reformist, moderate, and principlist — that compete in elections with established party platforms and voter registration rolls.
choices.explanation: The CED explicitly states Iran lacks formal political party structures; the factions described are loosely formed political alliances, not formal parties with registration, platforms, and organizational infrastructure.

choices.text: Iran's Majles operates without formal political parties; deputies instead align with loosely formed political alliances that have questionable linkage to constituents.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-4.B.1.b, Iran's legislative politics is organized around shifting political alliances rather than formal parties with established structures, voter bases, and policy platforms.

choices.text: Iran has a one-party system in which the Islamic Republican Party controls all legislative seats.
choices.explanation: Iran's formal party system collapsed after the Islamic Republican Party dissolved in 1987; subsequent politics has been organized through factional alliances, not a formal one-party structure.

choices.text: Iran's political parties are identical to Mexico's PAN, PRD, and PRI in their organizational structure and functions.
choices.explanation: Mexico's parties have formal organizational structures, national platforms, and established voter registration; Iran's political alliances explicitly lack these features per the CED.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Critics of single-member district systems argue that voters in districts where their preferred candidate has no realistic chance of winning face a strategic choice: vote sincerely for their preferred candidate (and likely waste that vote), or vote for the 'lesser evil' — a candidate they prefer less but who can actually win. This strategic behavior is sometimes called 'tactical voting.'"

questions.question: Which of the following best explains how tactical voting in the UK illustrates a limitation of FPTP highlighted in the course?

choices.text: Tactical voting shows that FPTP systems fail to produce majority governments, forcing parties into coalition negotiations after every election.
choices.explanation: FPTP tends to produce majority or near-majority governments by amplifying the seat share of plurality winners; coalition governments are more associated with PR systems that produce fragmented parliaments.

choices.text: Tactical voting shows FPTP pressures voters to vote against options rather than for preferences, producing wasted votes and distorted representation.
choices.explanation: Per DEM-2.B.2, FPTP produces wasted votes and strategic voting dilemmas — when a voter's sincere preference has no chance of winning locally, they face pressure to vote tactically; this is a direct consequence of the plurality-winner structure and illustrates the representational cost of FPTP.

choices.text: Tactical voting shows that FPTP produces the same representational outcomes as PR systems because voters self-correct for disproportionality.
choices.explanation: Tactical voting is an imperfect coping mechanism, not a correction; it does not eliminate FPTP's disproportionality and in fact reflects a representational failure that PR systems reduce by making sincere voting more effective.

choices.text: Tactical voting is a feature of Iran's system where Guardian Council vetting forces voters to choose among pre-approved candidates rather than their preferred candidates.
choices.explanation: Iran's candidate constraint is imposed by the Guardian Council before the ballot is formed, not by voter strategic calculation under FPTP mechanics; tactical voting in the passage's sense is an FPTP phenomenon.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "A passage from a comparative government textbook: 'The strength and diversity of civil society is not merely a product of democratization — it is also one of its causes. Countries with robust networks of voluntary associations, independent media, and social movements create the social infrastructure of democratic culture: citizens who know how to organize, who distrust monopolies of power, and who have experience holding institutions accountable.'"

questions.question: Which of the following best uses refutation, concession, or rebuttal to evaluate the author's argument that strong civil society causes democratization?

choices.text: The argument is completely correct because Mexico's Zapatistas and indigenous groups directly and solely caused Mexico's democratic transition in 2000 — without them, no institutional reforms would have occurred.
choices.explanation: Conceding the argument entirely without qualification overstates the case; Mexico's democratic transition involved multiple institutional reforms (el dedazo elimination, INE creation, privatization) and was not solely caused by the Zapatistas.

choices.text: Mexico supports the claim, but Russia — which has dismantled civil society through NGO restrictions and managed elections — shows the relationship runs both ways.
choices.explanation: Per IEF-2 and PAU-4.A.3, the author's claim has merit in Mexico's case (civil society and democratization reinforced each other), but Russia shows that an authoritarian regime can reverse civil society autonomy through NGO restrictions and managed democracy — the relationship is bidirectional.

choices.text: The argument is fully refuted because China has an extremely active civil society through its mass organizations yet has not democratized.
choices.explanation: China's mass organizations are state-directed, not autonomous civil society; they do not embody the 'voluntary associations and independent media' the author describes — China does not refute the argument because it lacks the independent civil society the claim requires.

choices.text: The argument is irrelevant because party systems, not civil society, are the primary cause of democratization in all course countries.
choices.explanation: Civil society and social movements are explicitly treated as relevant to democratization in IEF-2; dismissing the argument as irrelevant ignores the CED's explicit treatment of civil society as a factor in democratic development.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Nigeria's National Assembly consists of two chambers: a House of Representatives with 360 members directly elected from single-member districts, and a Senate with 109 members — three from each of Nigeria's 36 states, plus one from the Federal Capital Territory. Two major parties — the PDP and APC — have alternated control of both chambers since Nigeria's return to civilian rule in 1999."

questions.question: Which of the following best explains how the pattern described in the passage illustrates Nigeria's multiparty system despite having more than 30 registered parties?

choices.text: FPTP districts and the state-based Senate reward national organizational reach, systematically advantaging established major parties over smaller competitors.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-4.A.5.a and DEM-2.B.2, FPTP's winner-take-all district rules and the need to win across diverse states (geographic breadth) advantage established parties with national organizational capacity; over 30 parties register but only PDP and APC can mobilize the nationwide infrastructure needed to win most seats.

choices.text: Nigeria prohibits parties other than PDP and APC from registering, which explains why they dominate despite 30 registered parties.
choices.explanation: Nigeria has over 30 registered parties; PDP and APC dominance is a competitive outcome of FPTP electoral dynamics and organizational advantages, not a legal prohibition on other parties.

choices.text: Nigeria's Senate uses proportional representation, which allocates three seats per state proportionally to PDP and APC based on national vote shares.
choices.explanation: Nigeria's Senate allocates three seats per state through direct elections, not PR; the three seats are not distributed proportionally by party — they are individually elected.

choices.text: Nigeria is a one-party system because PDP and APC have monopolized power since 1999, leaving no room for genuine third-party competition.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-4.A.5.a, Nigeria is a multiparty system; the CED explicitly notes a third party achieving some electoral success, and the presence of over 30 registered parties confirms this is not a one-party system.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "An excerpt from a comparative politics argument: 'Whether an authoritarian regime should be classified as using a one-party system or a dominant-party managed democracy matters analytically. In a one-party system, legal prohibition ensures no rival can compete. In a dominant-party managed democracy, the appearance of competition makes the regime seem more legitimate internationally and may even provide useful information about societal preferences — but the outcome is engineered."

questions.question: Which of the following best explains the practical significance of the analytical distinction the author draws for understanding Russia versus China?

choices.text: The distinction is practically significant because Russia's managed elections provide opposition parties with real power in a way China's one-party system does not.
choices.explanation: Russia's opposition parties do not gain real power through managed elections; the distinction the author draws is about analytical classification and the regime's legitimation strategy, not about opposition parties actually gaining power.

choices.text: Russia uses elections as a legitimating facade; China makes no competitive claim at all — different strategies requiring distinct analytical frameworks.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-4.A.2 and PAU-4.A.3, China openly uses legal prohibition; Russia uses formal elections as legitimation tools — the analytical distinction helps explain why Russia tolerates opposition registration while China does not, and why international assessments of their electoral systems should differ.

choices.text: The distinction is not analytically significant because both systems produce the same outcome: indefinite incumbent dominance.
choices.explanation: Producing similar outcomes does not make the mechanisms analytically equivalent; understanding how dominance is produced — legal prohibition versus managed competition — reveals different vulnerabilities, legitimation strategies, and reform pathways.

choices.text: China's one-party system is analytically identical to Russia's managed democracy because the CPC and United Russia both use threshold rules to limit opposition.
choices.explanation: Threshold rules are specific to electoral systems; China's NPC selection does not use PR thresholds — conflating the two mechanisms misrepresents China's one-party legal structure.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Nigeria's federal legislature contains provisions designed to ensure ethnic and regional representation: party candidates for certain offices must meet geographic distribution requirements, ethnic group membership is factored into party ticket construction, and the federal character principle — that major institutions must reflect Nigeria's diversity — shapes appointments and representation."

questions.question: Which of the following best explains how ethnic quotas and the federal character principle affect party competition in Nigeria?

choices.text: Cross-ethnic coalition-building is structurally required in Nigeria, producing a competitive logic unlike the UK's ideologically driven party competition.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-4.B.1.d, Nigeria's ethnic quotas and federal character principle mean that successful parties must aggregate across ethnic and regional lines — a different competitive logic than the UK's class/ideology-based party competition, where ethnicity is not a formal representational criterion.

choices.text: Ethnic quotas effectively eliminate competition in Nigeria because parties know in advance which ethnic groups will win designated seats.
choices.explanation: Ethnic representation requirements shape the composition of candidate slates and party tickets, but they do not eliminate electoral competition; parties still compete within these constraints for voter support.

choices.text: The federal character principle converts Nigeria into a one-party state because no single party can meet all ethnic representation requirements.
choices.explanation: The federal character principle is a representational norm, not a bar to competition; Nigeria has two major competing parties plus a third, not a one-party system, demonstrating that cross-ethnic requirements can be met competitively.

choices.text: Ethnic quotas in Nigeria function identically to gender quotas in Mexico, both producing identical party system outcomes.
choices.explanation: Both mechanisms promote inclusion of underrepresented groups, but they operate in different contexts — Mexico's gender quotas target women's representation in party lists, while Nigeria's ethnic provisions address multi-ethnic federal representation; the party system outcomes differ as well.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "A student argues: 'Electoral systems are the most important factor in determining whether a country has a democratic party system. Change the electoral rules, and you change the party system and ultimately the degree of democratization.'"

questions.question: Which of the following provides the strongest refutation of the student's argument using evidence from two course countries?

choices.text: Russia and Mexico use similar mixed SMD+PR systems yet produce opposite outcomes: Mexico has genuine multiparty competition while Russia has engineered dominance — electoral rules alone do not determine outcomes.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-4.A.3 and PAU-4.A.4, Mexico and Russia both use mixed SMD + PR systems, yet Mexico has genuine multiparty competition while Russia has engineered United Russia dominance; the difference lies not in electoral formula but in the presence or absence of managed democracy tools — directly refuting electoral rules as the primary determinant.

choices.text: The UK uses FPTP and has a two-party dominant system with regional exceptions — this confirms the electoral-rules-determine-outcomes claim and therefore cannot serve as a refutation.
choices.explanation: This confirms rather than refutes the student's claim; the question asks for a refutation — a case where electoral rules did not determine the party system outcome.

choices.text: China uses an indirect election system and is a one-party state, which superficially aligns with the claim that electoral design determines party outcomes — but the one-party rule is a product of law, not indirect election mechanics.
choices.explanation: China's one-party system is primarily the result of CPC legal prohibition, not of its indirect election rules; moreover, this confirms rather than refutes the claim.

choices.text: Nigeria uses FPTP and has a multiparty system, while the UK uses FPTP and has a two-party dominant system — superficially suggesting no relationship, but this overstates the case in the opposite direction.
choices.explanation: This comparison shows the relationship is not deterministic, which partially supports a refutation, but 'no relationship exists' is itself an overstatement; electoral rules are a factor — just not the only factor — and the best refutation shows a case where the same rule produces different outcomes due to non-electoral regime mechanisms.
